statements.impact_statement: GNU tar path-traversal via crafted archives requires the system tar binary to extract untrusted archives. The vectorizer entrypoint execs the Rust binary directly and never shells out to /usr/bin/tar; archive handling (.vecdb snapshots) uses the Rust `tar` crate compiled into the binary. The deb tar package exists in the base only for operator debugging via docker exec.

Historic tar setuid/setgid-preservation behaviour (disputed, 2005). The runtime never invokes system tar; container runs as nonroot UID 65532, so preserved setuid bits from an extraction could not grant elevation anyway.

Disputed uncontrolled-recursion in glibc regcomp with crafted patterns. The vectorizer binary does not pass untrusted input to POSIX regcomp/regexec; Rust regex handling uses the `regex` crate (no glibc regex). No network-reachable path feeds patterns to glibc.

Alleged stack-guard bypass, disputed by glibc upstream as not a vulnerability. Binary is compiled with stack protectors and runs as nonroot; no adversary-controlled native code execution path exists in the container.
Disputed ldd/ld.so code-execution when running ldd on a crafted binary. Nothing in the runtime executes ldd, and no untrusted executables are loaded; the container runs a single fixed binary.

Alleged ASLR-mmap weakness, disputed by upstream. Exploitation requires an existing memory-corruption primitive in the process; none is exposed, and the finding is informational at LOW severity with no fix planned in trixie.
Alleged thread-stack address-guessing hardening gap, disputed by upstream as not a vulnerability. Requires a separate memory-disclosure primitive to matter; no such surface is exposed by the runtime.

Uncontrolled recursion in glibc regexec with a crafted pattern (denial of service). No code path passes adversary-controlled patterns to glibc regex functions; the binary's regex engine is the Rust `regex` crate.

glob() denial-of-service with crafted patterns, relevant to FTP-style servers exposing glob to remote users. The vectorizer never exposes glibc glob() to network input; file discovery uses Rust globset/walkdir compiled into the binary.

systemd-journald forgery issue (disputed). systemd is not PID 1 in the container and journald never runs — the image entrypoint directly execs the vectorizer binary. Only libsystemd0 shared-library files are present as a dependency of other deb packages; the vulnerable daemon code never executes.
systemd-journald sealing bypass (disputed). No journald process exists at runtime; the container has no systemd init. Vulnerable code is present on disk only and is never executed.
systemd-journald FSS sealing weakness (disputed). Journald is never started in the container; logging goes to stdout/stderr and is collected by the container runtime.

systemd TOCTOU when updating file permissions during systemd-managed service startup. The container does not run systemd as init and no systemd unit files are processed; the vectorizer process is exec'd directly by the container runtime.

coreutils sort heap-overflow via crafted key spec. The runtime never invokes coreutils binaries; they exist in the base image solely for interactive docker exec debugging by operators, who control their own inputs.
chown/chgrp TOCTOU race in coreutils. No runtime code path executes chown/chgrp; file ownership is fixed at image build time via COPY --chown, and the process runs as a single non-root user.
